title: Observation of hybrid higher-order skin-topological effect in non-Hermitian topolectrical circuits
type1_summary: One Excel workbook containing figure-organized tabular source data for spectra and voltage distributions, directly replot-ready.
type2_summary: No clear raw/unprocessed instrument-output files were provided in the inspected dataset.

classification.reasoning: This file is organized as cleaned, figure-specific tabular data with physical-variable-like columns and sheet names matching the paper's figures. Although some sheets correspond to experimentally measured spectra/voltage distributions, the workbook structure indicates curated source data prepared for plotting. It is not a raw instrument file and can be replot-ready with minimal processing.
classification.paper_evidence: The paper describes these figures as measured spectra and voltage distributions used for publication figures, and the deposit is organized by figure panels (Fig. 2, Fig. 4, Fig. 6), consistent with source/figure data rather than raw instrument output.
classification.file_evidence: Excel workbook with 8 figure-specific sheets named after published panels (e.g., 'Fig. 4(a)', 'Fig. 4(b)', 'Fig. 2(a)', 'Fig. 2(b)', 'Fig. 2(c)-(d), Fig. 3(c)-(d)', 'Fig. 4(c)-(d)', 'Fig. 6(d)', 'Fig. 6(e)'). Each sheet has tabular numeric data with headers and moderate size (181.5 KB), suitable for direct plotting.
classification.ambiguity: Low ambiguity: the paper and file structure both indicate curated figure/source data, not raw acquisition output.
classification.key_columns: Figure-specific sheets; headers include 'The experimentally measured spectra for ST effect', 'The experimentally measured spectra for SS effect', 'The theoretical measured spectra for ST effect', 'The theoretical measured spectra for SS effect', and voltage-distribution tables.

paper_analysis.summary: This paper demonstrates hybrid higher-order skin-topological states in non-Hermitian topolectrical circuits, using 2D and 3D LC/INIC circuit lattices to realize selective skin localization acting only on topological boundary modes. The key finding is experimental observation of 2D skin-topological (ST), 2D skin-skin (SS), 3D skin-topological-topological (STT), and 3D skin-skin-skin (SSS) boundary states, confirmed by voltage maps and admittance spectra near resonance.
measurement_types: AC voltage mapping on topolectrical circuits

figures.description: Schematic of the 2D topolectrical circuit network with 6×6 unit cells and four sublattices per cell, showing capacitive/inductive couplings and INIC-based nonreciprocal links.
Unit cell design for realizing the hybrid second-order skin-topological (ST) phase.
Unit cell design for realizing the skin-skin (SS) phase.
Detailed INIC circuit implementations used to generate different non-Hermitian couplings.
Simulated circuit-Laplacian spectra for the 2D ST case under different boundary conditions (double periodic, x-periodic/y-open, double open), showing selective spectral change of topological modes.
Simulated circuit-Laplacian spectra for the 2D SS case under different boundary conditions, showing global spectral change characteristic of skin effect in both directions.
Simulated voltage distribution at resonance for the 2D ST case, showing corner-localized voltages with sublattice-selective amplitudes.
Simulated voltage distribution at resonance for the 2D SS case, showing corner-localized voltages with nearly equal sublattice amplitudes.
Photograph of the fabricated 2D circuit sample for the ST effect.
Photograph of the fabricated 2D circuit sample for the SS effect.
Measured voltage distribution at resonance for the 2D ST circuit.
Measured voltage distribution at resonance for the 2D SS circuit.
Measured spectra for the fabricated 2D ST circuit under different boundary conditions.
Measured spectra for the fabricated 2D SS circuit under different boundary conditions.
Measured voltage versus driving frequency for corner, edge, and bulk sites in the 2D ST circuit.
Measured voltage versus driving frequency for corner, edge, and bulk sites in the 2D SS circuit.
3D circuit unit cell design for the hybrid STT case, with z-direction nonreciprocal couplings added to stacked 2D topological layers.
3D circuit unit cell design for the SSS case, with nonreciprocity along x, y, and z directions.
Simulated voltage distribution at resonance for the 3D STT case.
Simulated voltage distribution at resonance for the 3D SSS case.
Photograph of the fabricated 3D circuit sample.
Enlarged unit cell view for the 3D STT circuit.
Enlarged unit cell view for the 3D SSS circuit.
Measured voltage distribution at resonance for the 3D STT circuit.
Measured voltage distribution at resonance for the 3D SSS circuit.

paper_analysis.raw_measurement_details: The paper reports experimental voltage measurements on fabricated 2D and 3D topolectrical circuits. Raw experimental observables are node voltages measured under AC excitation at resonance and as a function of driving frequency, plus admittance-band measurements reconstructed from repeated node-by-node excitation/response measurements. Photographs of fabricated samples are also shown.
paper_analysis.processed_plot_details: Processed plots include simulated and measured spectra of the circuit Laplacian/admittance under different boundary conditions, voltage distributions at resonance, and frequency-dependent voltage amplitudes at corner/edge/bulk sites. These are derived from measured voltage-response matrices and/or LTspice simulations rather than direct raw sensor output.

dataset_characterization.data_availability_statement: The paper states that any related experimental background information not mentioned in the text and other findings are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request, and that the data generated in the study are provided in the Source data file.
dataset_characterization.raw_data_description: The text does not describe release of raw voltage traces, raw node-by-node response matrices, or raw instrument files. It only states that data generated in the study are provided in the Source data file and that additional information is available on request.
dataset_characterization.processed_data_description: Processed data include measured voltage distributions, frequency-dependent voltage curves, and spectra/admittance bands reconstructed from measured response matrices. The paper also includes LTspice simulation outputs and theoretical spectra/voltage maps.
dataset_characterization.figure_data_description: Yes. The paper explicitly says the generated data are provided in the Source data file, which in Nature Communications typically corresponds to figure/source data supporting the published plots. The text does not specify file names or per-figure folder structure.
dataset_characterization.scripts_description: No analysis scripts, notebooks, or code repositories are mentioned in the provided text.
dataset_characterization.data_organization: figure-based source data is implied, but exact organization is not specified
dataset_characterization.replot_reason: The paper states that source data are provided, and the main figures are based on measurable quantities such as spectra and voltage maps. However, the text does not specify whether the source data are raw matrices or already processed figure values, so replotting is likely possible for published figures but exact reproducibility from raw data is unclear.
dataset_characterization.notes: The dataset is primarily experimental circuit-response data plus simulation outputs. The most important semantic distinction is that the published 'Source data' are likely figure-supporting processed values rather than raw oscilloscope/instrument exports. The paper also describes a measurement protocol where the full response matrix G is reconstructed by repeating node excitation measurements (144 times for open boundaries, 24 times for x-periodic/y-open, 4 times for fully periodic), then inverted to obtain the circuit Laplacian and admittance bands.
